title: The Enchanted Scroll
subtitle: Phishing Attack Leading to Network Compromise
description: A cunning enchantment disguised as a routine missive has ensnared one of your realm's trusted servants. The deception has breached your outer defences, granting dark forces a foothold within the fortress walls. As the scope of the infiltration becomes clear, the race begins to purge the corruption before the enemy plunders your treasury.

roles.name: Incident Commander
roles.title: War Chief
roles.description: Leads the response team, makes containment and escalation decisions

roles.name: IT Operations Lead
roles.title: Arcane Engineer
roles.description: Provides technical context, assesses system impact, manages endpoint containment

roles.name: SOC Analyst
roles.title: Shadow Watcher
roles.description: Analyses phishing artefacts, traces lateral movement, monitors for further compromise

roles.name: Compliance / DPO
roles.title: Keeper of the Codex
roles.description: Assesses data exposure, regulatory notification obligations under GDPR and FCA rules

roles.name: Communications Lead
roles.title: Town Crier
roles.description: Drafts internal warnings to staff, manages external communications if required

roles.name: HR Representative
roles.title: Guild Master
roles.description: Supports affected staff, advises on conduct aspects if negligence is suspected

roles.name: Senior Management
roles.title: High Council Elder
roles.description: Provides executive decision authority, approves client notifications

injects.title: The Poisoned Missive — A Lure Takes Hold
injects.narrative:
It is 09:15 UTC on a Monday morning. The IT Service Desk receives a call from a relationship manager in the Wealth Management division. They report unusual behaviour on their workstation after clicking a link in an email that appeared to come from a well-known financial data provider. The email subject line was 'Urgent: Updated KYC Requirements — Action Required Today' and contained a link to what appeared to be a document portal.

Upon clicking, the user was briefly shown a fake Microsoft 365 login page where they entered their corporate credentials. The page then redirected to the legitimate provider's website, making the user believe it was a routine login.

The SOC's email gateway logs show the same phishing email was delivered to 47 recipients across the bank between 08:30 and 09:00 UTC. Preliminary analysis shows the email passed SPF and DKIM checks because the sending domain was a lookalike — 'financialdata-provider.com' rather than 'financialdataprovider.com'. Six users have clicked the link so far based on proxy logs.
facilitator_prompts: What is the immediate response? Do we block the sending domain and quarantine all copies of the email?
Six users have clicked — do we assume all six sets of credentials are compromised?
Should we force a password reset for the six users immediately, or for all 47 recipients as a precaution?
What additional telemetry should the SOC review to determine if the stolen credentials have been used?
How do we communicate to staff without causing panic — and do we need to warn about further phishing attempts?
Is this a reportable incident under our regulatory framework at this stage?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine whether the stolen credentials have already been used by the attacker

critical_fail.title: Total Compromise
critical_fail.description: The attacker used the first set of stolen credentials within minutes. They accessed the user's mailbox via IMAP from an overseas IP, set up mail forwarding rules to exfiltrate all incoming messages, and used the mailbox to send a second wave of highly targeted spear-phishing emails to 12 UHNW clients directly, posing as their relationship manager.
critical_fail.modifier: Client-facing attack launched from compromised mailbox. Reputational crisis imminent.

fail.title: Mailbox Access Confirmed
fail.description: Azure AD sign-in logs confirm that three of the six compromised accounts were accessed from a VPN exit node in Eastern Europe within 30 minutes of credential capture. The attacker downloaded email attachments containing client portfolio summaries and upcoming transaction details.
fail.modifier: Three mailboxes accessed. Client data exfiltrated. Breach notification likely required.

partial.title: Attempted Access Detected
partial.description: Conditional access policies blocked sign-in attempts from two unrecognised locations. However, one account with a legacy authentication exemption was successfully accessed for approximately 10 minutes before the session was terminated.
partial.modifier: Conditional access partially effective. One account breached briefly.

success.title: Defences Hold
success.description: Multi-factor authentication prevented all external login attempts. The attacker has the passwords but cannot bypass MFA. However, the credentials could be used in password-spraying attacks against other services that may not enforce MFA.
success.modifier: MFA blocked access. Credential rotation still required as a precaution.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — Threat Intelligence Windfall
critical_success.description: MFA blocked all access attempts. Additionally, the SOC's automated threat intelligence platform has correlated the phishing infrastructure with a known threat actor group that has been targeting financial services firms across Europe. NCSC has an active advisory on this campaign. The bank is now ahead of the threat.
critical_success.modifier: No compromise achieved. Threat actor identified. Intelligence shared with sector peers.

random_complications: One of the six users who clicked the link is the CISO's executive assistant, whose mailbox contains sensitive security programme documents and board reports.
The phishing email was also forwarded internally by a helpful team leader to their entire department (23 additional people) with the note 'Please action this today.'
The bank's email filtering vendor confirms a misconfiguration meant URL rewriting was disabled for the past 72 hours, so safe-links protection was not active.
A second phishing campaign — unrelated to the first — is detected targeting the Finance team with fake invoice attachments containing macro-enabled malware.

injects.title: The Spreading Blight — Lateral Movement Detected
injects.narrative:
The SOC's investigation escalates. Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) telemetry reveals that one of the compromised users — a senior relationship manager — had their workstation accessed remotely by the threat actor using legitimate remote desktop tools. The attacker installed a commercial remote access tool (AnyDesk) on the workstation and used it to move laterally within the network.

The attacker has enumerated Active Directory, identified service accounts, and attempted to access the CRM database server. The CRM system holds personal data, financial records, and source of funds documentation for 3,200 clients.

Additionally, the compromised user had saved several passwords in their browser, including credentials for the bank's client onboarding portal and a third-party anti-money laundering screening service.
facilitator_prompts: The attacker has moved from email compromise to endpoint compromise. How does this change our containment strategy?
Should we isolate the entire Wealth Management network segment, even if it disrupts business operations?
What is the exposure if the CRM database was accessed? Do we know if the attacker succeeded?
Saved browser passwords have been compromised. How do we identify and rotate all affected credentials?
The third-party AML screening service credentials are compromised. Do we notify the third party?
At what point do we engage external incident response support and/or law enforcement?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine the extent of CRM database access

critical_fail.title: Full Database Exfiltration
critical_fail.description: Network flow analysis confirms a large data transfer from the CRM database server to an external IP address. Approximately 2.8 GB of data — the entire client database including PII, financial records, and KYC documents — has been exfiltrated. Evidence suggests the data is already being offered for sale on a dark web marketplace.
critical_fail.modifier: Full client database exfiltrated. Mandatory breach notification to ICO within 72 hours. All 3,200 clients must be notified.

fail.title: Partial Access Achieved
fail.description: The attacker successfully authenticated to the CRM using the saved browser credentials and executed several database queries. Export logs show approximately 400 client records were accessed, focused on UHNW clients with balances exceeding £5 million. Data exfiltration cannot be confirmed or ruled out.
fail.modifier: 400 UHNW client records potentially compromised. Targeted data theft suspected.

partial.title: Access Attempted But Limited
partial.description: The attacker authenticated to the CRM but the database access was read-only at the application tier. Several queries were executed but the application's data export functionality was restricted by role-based access controls. Some client names and contact details may have been viewed on screen.
partial.modifier: Limited CRM access. Data exfiltration unlikely but viewing of records confirmed.

success.title: CRM Access Blocked
success.description: The CRM system's IP allowlist prevented the attacker from authenticating from the compromised workstation, which was on a different network segment. The saved credentials are compromised but were not successfully used.
success.modifier: CRM protected by network controls. No client data accessed via CRM.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — Attacker Trapped
critical_success.description: The CRM's anomaly detection system flagged the unusual login attempt and triggered a honeypot response, feeding the attacker fabricated client data while alerting the SOC. The deception technology has captured the attacker's tools, techniques, and infrastructure details, providing invaluable threat intelligence.
critical_success.modifier: Attacker deceived by honeypot. Full TTP profile captured. No real data compromised.

random_complications: The AnyDesk remote access tool was installed using a legitimate IT admin account, making it initially indistinguishable from authorised remote support sessions.
The compromised AML screening service account was shared among 15 staff members, and the third-party provider's logs show access from multiple suspicious IPs over the past week.

A client calls their relationship manager to query a suspicious email they received — it appears the attacker is using compromised mailbox data to target clients directly with business email compromise.
The EDR platform reaches its maximum isolation capacity and begins queuing endpoints, leaving some compromised machines active on the network.

injects.title: The Reckoning — Containment and Recovery
injects.narrative:
Twelve hours into the incident, containment is underway but significant challenges remain. The threat actor has been ejected from the network, all compromised credentials have been rotated, and affected endpoints are being rebuilt.

However, the following issues require immediate executive attention:

1. The ICO notification deadline is approaching. The DPO must determine whether this constitutes a notifiable personal data breach and prepare the submission.

2. Three UHNW clients have independently contacted the bank reporting suspicious emails and phone calls that reference details only the bank would hold. The attacker appears to be using stolen data to conduct social engineering attacks against clients.

3. The bank's cyber insurance broker is requesting a detailed timeline of events. Early indications from the insurer suggest that the phishing email bypassed controls because of a known misconfiguration, which may affect coverage.

4. A Financial Times journalist has contacted the communications team asking about 'a significant cyber incident at the bank' — the source of the leak is unknown.
facilitator_prompts: What is the ICO notification strategy? Do we have sufficient information to file within 72 hours?
How do we protect clients who are now being targeted by the attacker using stolen bank data?
What is our media response strategy? Do we issue a proactive statement or respond only to direct inquiries?
How do we manage the insurance claim given the potential coverage dispute over the email misconfiguration?
What lessons have already emerged that should be addressed immediately, not deferred to a post-incident review?
How do we restore trust with affected clients and what remediation or compensation should be offered?

dice_events.description: Roll to determine the extent of client-facing impact from the stolen data

critical_fail.title: Client Losses
critical_fail.description: Two clients have fallen victim to business email compromise attacks using data stolen from the bank. A total of £1.4 million has been transferred to attacker-controlled accounts. The clients hold the bank responsible and are engaging solicitors. Media coverage is intensifying.
critical_fail.modifier: £1.4 million in client losses. Legal action initiated. Full media crisis.

fail.title: Widespread Client Targeting
fail.description: Over 50 clients report receiving suspicious communications. While no financial losses have occurred yet, several clients are demanding to close their accounts. The bank's client retention team is overwhelmed.
fail.modifier: Mass client contact required. Significant client attrition risk.

partial.title: Contained Impact
partial.description: The bank's proactive outreach to potentially affected clients has been well-received. Some clients are concerned but appreciate the transparency. Three clients request enhanced security measures on their accounts.
partial.modifier: Client impact manageable through proactive communication.

success.title: Effective Client Protection
success.description: The rapid client notification campaign, combined with temporary enhanced authentication measures, has prevented any client losses. Feedback from clients is largely positive, praising the bank's swift and transparent response.
success.modifier: No client losses. Reputation preserved through strong response.

critical_success.title: Critical Success — Strengthened Relationships
critical_success.description: The bank's exemplary incident handling becomes a case study in effective breach response. Several prospects cite the transparent handling as a reason to move their assets to the bank. The FCA commends the bank's response in its next supervisory letter.
critical_success.modifier: Reputational gain from excellent incident handling. Regulatory commendation.

random_complications: The original phishing email is discovered to contain a zero-day exploit in addition to the credential harvesting link. Patching is not yet available.
An internal investigation reveals that mandatory phishing awareness training completion rates were only 62%, and the board had not been informed of this gap.
The journalist's source is identified as a disgruntled former employee who learned of the incident through contacts still at the bank.
The cyber insurance policy excess is £500,000 and the insurer is disputing the claim, leaving the bank to cover all incident response costs in the interim.

debrief.title: Post-Battle Assessment
questions: Were email security controls adequate to detect and block the phishing campaign?
Was the incident escalation pathway clear and timely?
Were credential compromise procedures effective — including MFA enforcement and password rotation?
How effective was internal communication to staff during the incident?
Were regulatory notification obligations understood and met within required timelines?
What improvements to phishing awareness training are needed?
